title: Introduction to Beta Kernel Model
text: Beta Kernel Process (BKP) provides flexible computationally efficient nonparametric framework modeling spatially varying binomial probabilities. Let x→=(x1,x2,…,xd)∈𝒳⊂ℝd\vec{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d) \\mathcal{X} \subset \mathbb{R}^d denote dd-dimensional input, suppose success probability surface π(x→)∈[0,1]\pi(\vec{x}) \[0,1] unknown. location x→\vec{x}, observed data modeled y(x→)∼Binomial(m(x→),π(x→)), y(\vec{x}) \sim \mathrm{Binomial}(m(\vec{x}), \pi(\vec{x})), y(x→)y(\vec{x}) number successes m(x→)m(\vec{x}) independent trials. full dataset comprises nn observations 𝒟n={(x→,yi,mi)}=1n\mathcal{D}_n = \{(\vec{x}_i, y_i, m_i)\}_{=1}^n, write yi=y(x→)y_i= y(\vec{x}_i) mi=m(x→)m_i= m(\vec{x}_i) brevity.

title: Introduction to Beta Kernel Model
text: line Bayesian paradigm, assign Beta prior unknown probability function: π(x→)∼Beta(α0(x→),β0(x→)), \pi(\vec{x}) \sim \mathrm{Beta}(\alpha_0(\vec{x}), \beta_0(\vec{x})), α0(x→)>0\alpha_0(\vec{x}) > 0 β0(x→)>0\beta_0(\vec{x}) > 0 spatially varying shape parameters.

title: Introduction to Beta Kernel Model
text: Let k:𝒳×𝒳→[0,1]k: \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \[0,1] denote user-defined kernel function measuring similarity input locations. kernel-based Bayesian updating strategy, BKP model defines closed-form posterior distribution π(x→)\pi(\vec{x}) π(x→)∣𝒟n∼Beta(αn(x→),βn(x→)), \pi(\vec{x}) \mid \mathcal{D}_n \sim \mathrm{Beta}\left(\alpha_n(\vec{x}), \beta_n(\vec{x})\right), αn(x→)=α0(x→)+∑=1nk(x→,x→)yi=α0(x→)+k→(x→)⊤y→, \alpha_n(\vec{x}) = \alpha_0(\vec{x}) + \sum_{=1}^{n} k(\vec{x}, \vec{x}_i) y_i = \alpha_0(\vec{x}) + \vec{k}(\vec{x})^\top \vec{y}, βn(x→)=β0(x→)+∑=1nk(x→,x→)(mi−yi)=β0(x→)+k→(x→)⊤(m→−y→), \beta_n(\vec{x})  = \beta_0(\vec{x}) + \sum_{=1}^{n} k(\vec{x}, \vec{x}_i) (m_i - y_i) = \beta_0(\vec{x}) + \vec{k}(\vec{x})^\top (\vec{m} - \vec{y}), k→(x→)=[k(x→,x→1),…,k(x→,x→n)]⊤\vec{k}(\vec{x}) = [k(\vec{x}, \vec{x}_1), \ldots, k(\vec{x}, \vec{x}_n)]^\top vector kernel weights.

title: Introduction to Beta Kernel Model
text: Based posterior distribution , posterior mean π̂n(x→)=αn(x→)αn(x→)+βn(x→), \widehat{\pi}_n(\vec{x}) = \frac{\alpha_n(\vec{x})}{\alpha_n(\vec{x}) + \beta_n(\vec{x})}, serves smooth estimator latent success probability. corresponding posterior variance sn2(x→)=π̂n(x→){1−π̂n(x→)}αn(x→)+βn(x→)+1, s^2_n(\vec{x}) = \frac{\widehat{\pi}_n(\vec{x})\{1 - \widehat{\pi}_n(\vec{x})\}} {\alpha_n(\vec{x}) + \beta_n(\vec{x}) + 1}, provides local measure epistemic uncertainty. posterior summaries can used visualize prediction quality across input space, particularly highlighting regions sparse data coverage (see Section @ref(sec:example) illustrations).

title: Introduction to Beta Kernel Model
text: binary classification, posterior mean can thresholded produce hard predictions: ŷ(x→)={1if π̂n(x→)>π0,0otherwise, \widehat{y}(\vec{x}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{} \widehat{\pi}_n(\vec{x}) > \pi_0, \\ 0 & \text{otherwise}, \end{cases} π0∈(0,1)\pi_0 \(0,1) user-specified threshold, typically set 0.50.5.

title: Introduction to Beta Kernel Model
text: Dirichlet Kernel Process (DKP) naturally extends BKP framework multi-class responses replacing binomial likelihood multinomial model Beta prior Dirichlet prior . Let response input x→∈𝒳⊂ℝd\vec{x} \\mathcal{X} \subset \mathbb{R}^d y→(x→)=(y1(x→),…,yq(x→)), \vec{y}(\vec{x}) = \left(y_1(\vec{x}), \ldots, y_q(\vec{x})\right),  ys(x→)y_s(\vec{x}) denotes count class ss m(x→)=∑s=1qys(x→)m(\vec{x}) = \sum_{s=1}^q y_s(\vec{x}) total trials. Assume y→(x→)∼Multinomial(m(x→),π→(x→)), \vec{y}(\vec{x}) \sim \mathrm{Multinomial}(m(\vec{x}), \vec{\pi}(\vec{x})),  class probabilities π→(x→)=(π1(x→),…,πq(x→)),∑s=1qπs(x→)=1. \vec{\pi}(\vec{x}) = (\pi_1(\vec{x}), \ldots, \pi_q(\vec{x})), \quad \sum_{s=1}^q \pi_s(\vec{x}) = 1.

title: Introduction to Beta Kernel Model
text: Dirichlet prior placed π→(x→)\vec{\pi}(\vec{x}): π→(x→)∼Dirichlet(α→0(x→)), \vec{\pi}(\vec{x}) \sim \mathrm{Dirichlet}(\vec{\alpha}_0(\vec{x})),  α→0(x→)=(α0,1(x→),…,α0,q(x→)) \vec{\alpha}_0(\vec{x}) = (\alpha_{0,1}(\vec{x}), \ldots, \alpha_{0,q}(\vec{x}))  prior concentration parameters.

title: Introduction to Beta Kernel Model
text: Given training data 𝒟n={(x→,y→)}=1n\mathcal{D}_n = \{(\vec{x}_i, \vec{y}_i)\}_{=1}^n, define response matrix Y→=[y→1,…,y→n]⊤∈ℝn×q. \vec{Y} = [\vec{y}_1, \ldots, \vec{y}_n]^\top \\mathbb{R}^{n \times q}. kernel-smoothed conjugate posterior distribution becomes π→(x→)∣𝒟n∼Dirichlet(α→n(x→)),withα→n(x→)=α→0(x→)+k→(x→)⊤Y→.\begin{equation}\label{eq:DKP_model} \vec{\pi}(\vec{x}) \mid \mathcal{D}_n \sim \mathrm{Dirichlet}\left(\vec{\alpha}_n(\vec{x})\right), \quad \text{} \quad \vec{\alpha}_n(\vec{x}) = \vec{\alpha}_0(\vec{x}) + \vec{k}(\vec{x})^\top \vec{Y}. \end{equation}

title: Introduction to Beta Kernel Model
text: posterior mean π̂n,s(x→)=αn,s(x→)∑s′=1qαn,s′(x→),s=1,…,q, \widehat{\pi}_{n,s}(\vec{x}) = \frac{\alpha_{n,s}(\vec{x})}{\sum_{s'=1}^q \alpha_{n,s'}(\vec{x})}, \quad s = 1, \ldots, q,  provides smooth estimate class probabilities.

title: Introduction to Beta Kernel Model
text: classification tasks, labels assigned maximum posteriori (MAP) decision rule: $$ \widehat{y}(\vec{x}) = \underset{s \\{1,\ldots,q\}}{\rm argmax}\; \widehat{\pi}_{n,s}(\vec{x}). $$

title: Authors and Citation
text: Jiangyan Zhao. Maintainer, author. Kunhai Qing. Author. Jin Xu. Author.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Zhao J, Qing K, Xu J (2025). BKP: Beta Dirichlet Kernel Processes Binomial Multinomial Modeling. R package version 0.1.0.9000, https://github.com/Jiangyan-Zhao/BKP.
code: @Manual{,   title = {BKP: Beta and Dirichlet Kernel Processes for Binomial and Multinomial Modeling},   author = {Jiangyan Zhao and Kunhai Qing and Jin Xu},   year = {2025},   note = {R package version 0.1.0.9000},   url = {https://github.com/Jiangyan-Zhao/BKP}, }

what: Beta and Dirichlet Kernel Processes for Binomial and Multinomial Modeling
title: Beta and Dirichlet Kernel Processes for Binomial and Multinomial Modeling
text: BKP package provides scalable Bayesian modeling tools binomial multinomial response data via Beta Kernel Process (BKP) generalization, Dirichlet Kernel Process (DKP). BKP designed binary (binomial) outcomes. DKP extends BKP handle multi-class responses via Dirichlet-multinomial modeling. models leverage kernel-based weighting conjugate priors enable efficient posterior inference, probabilistic prediction, uncertainty quantification.

title: Beta and Dirichlet Kernel Processes for Binomial and Multinomial Modeling
text: can install stable version BKP CRAN : install development version GitHub :
code: install.packages("BKP") # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("Jiangyan-Zhao/BKP")

title: Beta and Dirichlet Kernel Processes for Binomial and Multinomial Modeling
code: library(BKP)  # Simulate data set.seed(123) n <- 30 Xbounds <- matrix(c(-2, 2), nrow = 1) x <- matrix(seq(-2, 2, length.out = n), ncol = 1) true_pi <- (1 + exp(-x^2) * cos(10 * (1 - exp(-x)) / (1 + exp(-x)))) / 2 m <- sample(50:100, n, replace = TRUE) y <- rbinom(n, size = m, prob = true_pi)  # Fit BKP model model <- fit.BKP(x, y, m, Xbounds = Xbounds)  # Predict on new data Xnew <- matrix(seq(-2, 2, length.out = 100), ncol = 1) pred <- predict(model, Xnew)  # Plot results plot(model)

title: Beta and Dirichlet Kernel Processes for Binomial and Multinomial Modeling
text: DKP generalizes BKP multi-class settings using Dirichlet-multinomial model.

title: Beta and Dirichlet Kernel Processes for Binomial and Multinomial Modeling
code: # Simulate 3-class data set.seed(123) n <- 30 Xbounds <- matrix(c(-2, 2), nrow = 1) x <- matrix(seq(-2, 2, length.out = n), ncol = 1) pi1 <- (1 + exp(-x^2) * cos(10 * (1 - exp(-x)) / (1 + exp(-x)))) / 2 pi_true <- cbind(pi1/2, pi1/2, 1 - pi1)  m <- sample(50:100, n, replace = TRUE) Y <- t(sapply(1:n, function(i) rmultinom(1, size = m[i], prob = pi_true[i, ])))  # Fit DKP model model_dkp <- fit.DKP(x, Y, Xbounds = Xbounds)  # Predict on new input Xnew <- matrix(seq(-2, 2, length.out = 10), ncol = 1) pred_dkp <- predict(model_dkp, Xnew)  # Plot results plot(model_dkp)

title: Beta and Dirichlet Kernel Processes for Binomial and Multinomial Modeling
text: ✅ Bayesian modeling binomial multinomial count data ✅ Kernel-based local information sharing ✅ Posterior prediction uncertainty quantification ✅ Class label prediction using threshold MAP rule ✅ Simulation posterior (Beta Dirichlet) distributions

title: Beta and Dirichlet Kernel Processes for Binomial and Multinomial Modeling
text: can simulate posterior samples using:
code: # BKP posterior draws simulate(model, Xnew, n_sim = 5)  # DKP posterior draws simulate(model_dkp, Xnew, n_sim = 5)

title: Beta and Dirichlet Kernel Processes for Binomial and Multinomial Modeling
text: use package work, please cite accompanying methodology papers package documentation.

title: Beta and Dirichlet Kernel Processes for Binomial and Multinomial Modeling
text: BKP package active development. Contributions suggestions welcome via GitHub issues pull requests.

what: Beta and Dirichlet Kernel Process Modeling — BKP-package
title: Beta and Dirichlet Kernel Process Modeling — BKP-package
text: BKP package provides tools nonparametric modeling binary, binomial, multinomial response data using Beta Kernel Process (BKP) extension, Dirichlet Kernel Process (DKP). methods estimate latent probability surfaces localized kernel smoothing Bayesian framework. package includes functionality model fitting, probabilistic prediction uncertainty quantification, posterior simulation, visualization one- two-dimensional input spaces. also supports hyperparameter tuning flexible prior specification.

title: Beta and Dirichlet Kernel Process Modeling — BKP-package
text: Core functionality organized following groups: fit.BKP, fit.DKP Fit BKP DKP model (multi)binomial response data. predict.BKP, predict.DKP Perform posterior predictive inference new input locations, including predictive means, variances, credible intervals. Classification labels returned automatically observations represent single trials (.e., binary outcomes). simulate.BKP, simulate.DKP Draw simulated responses posterior predictive distribution fitted model. plot.BKP, plot.DKP Visualize model predictions uncertainty bands 1D 2D input spaces. summary.BKP, summary.DKP, print.BKP, print.DKP Summarize print details fitted BKP DKP model.

title: Beta and Dirichlet Kernel Process Modeling — BKP-package
text: Goetschalckx R, Poupart P, Hoey J (2011). Continuous Correlated Beta Processes. Proceedings Twenty-Second International Joint Conference Artificial Intelligence - Volume Volume Two, IJCAI’11, p. 1269-1274. AAAI Press. MacKenzie CA, Trafalis TB, Barker K (2014). Bayesian Beta Kernel Model Binary Classification Online Learning Problems. Statistical Analysis Data Mining: ASA Data Science Journal, 7(6), 434-449. Rolland P, Kavis , Singla , Cevher V (2019). Efficient learning smooth probability functions Bernoulli tests guarantees. Proceedings 36th International Conference Machine Learning, ICML 2019, 9-15 June 2019, Long Beach, California, USA, volume 97 Proceedings Machine Learning Research, pp. 5459-5467. PMLR.

what: Fit a Dirichlet Kernel Process (DKP) Model — fit.DKP
title: Fit a Dirichlet Kernel Process (DKP) Model — fit.DKP
text: Fits DKP model multinomial response data locally smoothing observed counts estimate latent Dirichlet parameters.

title: Fit a Dirichlet Kernel Process (DKP) Model — fit.DKP
code: fit.DKP(   X,   Y,   Xbounds = NULL,   prior = c("noninformative", "fixed", "adaptive"),   r0 = 2,   p0 = NULL,   kernel = c("gaussian", "matern52", "matern32"),   loss = c("brier", "log_loss"),   n_multi_start = NULL,   theta = NULL )

title: Fit a Dirichlet Kernel Process (DKP) Model — fit.DKP
text: X numeric input matrix size \(n \times d\), row represents covariate vector. Y Matrix observed multinomial counts, dimension \(n \times q\). Xbounds Optional \(d \times 2\) matrix specifying lower upper bounds input dimension. Used normalize inputs \([0,1]^d\). Xbounds NULL, input assumed already normalized, default bounds set \([0,1]^d\). prior Type prior use. One "noninformative", "fixed", "adaptive". r0 Global prior precision (used prior = "fixed" "adaptive"). p0 Global prior mean vector (used prior = "fixed"). Must length \(q\). kernel Kernel function local weighting. Choose "gaussian", "matern52", "matern32". loss Loss function kernel hyperparameter tuning. One "brier" (default) "log_loss". n_multi_start Number random initializations multi-start optimization. Default 10 × d. theta Optional. positive scalar numeric vector length equal input dimension d. specified, values used directly kernel lengthscale parameters, bypassing internal optimization procedure. NULL (default), kernel parameters optimized via (multi-start) L-BFGS-B minimize chosen loss function.

title: Fit a Dirichlet Kernel Process (DKP) Model — fit.DKP
text: list class "DKP" representing fitted DKP model, following components: theta_opt Optimized kernel hyperparameters (lengthscales). kernel Kernel function used, string. loss Loss function used hyperparameter tuning. loss_min Minimum loss value achieved kernel hyperparameter optimization. theta manually specified user, value set NA. X Original (unnormalized) input matrix size n × d. Xnorm Normalized input matrix scaled \([0,1]^d\). Xbounds Matrix specifying normalization bounds input dimension. Y Observed multinomial counts size n × q. prior Type prior used. r0 Prior precision parameter. p0 Prior mean (fixed priors). alpha0 Prior Dirichlet parameters input location (scalar matrix). alpha_n Posterior Dirichlet parameters kernel smoothing.

title: Fit a Dirichlet Kernel Process (DKP) Model — fit.DKP
code: #-------------------------- 1D Example --------------------------- set.seed(123)  # Define true class probability function (3-class) true_pi_fun <- function(X) {   p <- (1 + exp(-X^2) * cos(10 * (1 - exp(-X)) / (1 + exp(-X)))) / 2   return(matrix(c(p/2, p/2, 1 - p), nrow = length(p))) }  n <- 30 Xbounds <- matrix(c(-2, 2), nrow = 1) X <- tgp::lhs(n = n, rect = Xbounds) true_pi <- true_pi_fun(X) m <- sample(100, n, replace = TRUE)  # Generate multinomial responses Y <- t(sapply(1:n, function(i) rmultinom(1, size = m[i], prob = true_pi[i, ])))  # Fit DKP model model1 <- fit.DKP(X, Y, Xbounds = Xbounds) print(model1) #> -------------------------------------------------- #>            Dirichlet Kernel Process (DKP) Model         #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Number of observations (n):  30 #> Input dimensionality (d):    1 #> Output dimensionality (q):   3 #> Kernel type:                 gaussian #> Loss function used:          brier #> Optimized kernel parameters: 0.0407 #> Minimum achieved loss:       0.01007 #> Kernel parameters were obtained by optimization. #>  #> Prior specification: #>   Noninformative prior: Dirichlet(1,...,1). #> --------------------------------------------------   #-------------------------- 2D Example --------------------------- set.seed(123)  # Define latent function and transform to 3-class probabilities true_pi_fun <- function(X) {   if (is.null(nrow(X))) X <- matrix(X, nrow = 1)   m <- 8.6928; s <- 2.4269   x1 <- 4 * X[,1] - 2   x2 <- 4 * X[,2] - 2   a <- 1 + (x1 + x2 + 1)^2 *     (19 - 14*x1 + 3*x1^2 - 14*x2 + 6*x1*x2 + 3*x2^2)   b <- 30 + (2*x1 - 3*x2)^2 *     (18 - 32*x1 + 12*x1^2 + 48*x2 - 36*x1*x2 + 27*x2^2)   f <- (log(a * b) - m) / s   p <- pnorm(f)   return(matrix(c(p/2, p/2, 1 - p), nrow = length(p))) }  n <- 100 Xbounds <- matrix(c(0, 0, 1, 1), nrow = 2) X <- tgp::lhs(n = n, rect = Xbounds) true_pi <- true_pi_fun(X) m <- sample(100, n, replace = TRUE)  # Generate multinomial responses Y <- t(sapply(1:n, function(i) rmultinom(1, size = m[i], prob = true_pi[i, ])))  # Fit DKP model model2 <- fit.DKP(X, Y, Xbounds = Xbounds) print(model2) #> -------------------------------------------------- #>            Dirichlet Kernel Process (DKP) Model         #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Number of observations (n):  100 #> Input dimensionality (d):    2 #> Output dimensionality (q):   3 #> Kernel type:                 gaussian #> Loss function used:          brier #> Optimized kernel parameters: 0.1245, 0.0706 #> Minimum achieved loss:       0.01060 #> Kernel parameters were obtained by optimization. #>  #> Prior specification: #>   Noninformative prior: Dirichlet(1,...,1). #> --------------------------------------------------

what: Fit a Beta Kernel Process (BKP) Model — fit.BKP
title: Fit a Beta Kernel Process (BKP) Model — fit.BKP
text: Fits BKP model binomial binary response data via local kernel smoothing. model constructs flexible latent probability surface updating Beta priors using kernel-weighted observations.

title: Fit a Beta Kernel Process (BKP) Model — fit.BKP
code: fit.BKP(   X,   y,   m,   Xbounds = NULL,   prior = c("noninformative", "fixed", "adaptive"),   r0 = 2,   p0 = 0.5,   kernel = c("gaussian", "matern52", "matern32"),   loss = c("brier", "log_loss"),   n_multi_start = NULL,   theta = NULL )

title: Fit a Beta Kernel Process (BKP) Model — fit.BKP
text: X numeric input matrix size \(n \times d\), row represents covariate vector. y numeric vector observed successes (length n). m numeric vector total binomial trials (length n), corresponding y. Xbounds Optional \(d \times 2\) matrix specifying lower upper bounds input dimension. Used normalize inputs \([0,1]^d\). Xbounds NULL, input assumed already normalized, default bounds set \([0,1]^d\). prior Type prior use. One "noninformative", "fixed", "adaptive". r0 Global prior precision (used prior = "fixed" "adaptive"). p0 Global prior mean (used prior = "fixed"). kernel Kernel function local weighting. Choose "gaussian", "matern52", "matern32". loss Loss function kernel hyperparameter tuning. One "brier" (default) "log_loss". n_multi_start Number random initializations multi-start optimization. Default 10 × d. theta Optional. positive scalar numeric vector length equal input dimension d. specified, values used directly kernel lengthscale parameters, bypassing internal optimization procedure. NULL (default), kernel parameters optimized via (multi-start) L-BFGS-B minimize chosen loss function.

title: Fit a Beta Kernel Process (BKP) Model — fit.BKP
text: list class "BKP" containing fitted BKP model, following elements: theta_opt Optimized kernel hyperparameters (lengthscales). kernel Kernel function used, string. loss Loss function used hyperparameter tuning. loss_min Minimum loss value achieved optimization. loss_min Minimum loss value achieved kernel hyperparameter optimization. theta manually specified user, value set NA. X Original (unnormalized) input matrix size n × d. Xnorm Normalized input matrix scaled \([0,1]^d\). Xbounds Matrix specifying normalization bounds input dimension. y Observed success counts. m Observed binomial trial counts. prior Type prior used. r0 Prior precision parameter. p0 Prior mean (fixed priors). alpha0 Prior shape parameter \(\alpha_0(\mathbf{x})\), either scalar vector. beta0 Prior shape parameter \(\beta_0(\mathbf{x})\), either scalar vector. alpha_n Posterior shape parameter \(\alpha_n(\mathbf{x})\). beta_n Posterior shape parameter \(\beta_n(\mathbf{x})\).

title: Fit a Beta Kernel Process (BKP) Model — fit.BKP
code: #-------------------------- 1D Example --------------------------- set.seed(123)  # Define true success probability function true_pi_fun <- function(x) {   (1 + exp(-x^2) * cos(10 * (1 - exp(-x)) / (1 + exp(-x)))) / 2 }  n <- 30 Xbounds <- matrix(c(-2,2), nrow=1) X <- tgp::lhs(n = n, rect = Xbounds) true_pi <- true_pi_fun(X) m <- sample(100, n, replace = TRUE) y <- rbinom(n, size = m, prob = true_pi)  # Fit BKP model model1 <- fit.BKP(X, y, m, Xbounds=Xbounds) print(model1) #> -------------------------------------------------- #>            Beta Kernel Process (BKP) Model         #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Number of observations (n):  30 #> Input dimensionality (d):    1 #> Kernel type:                 gaussian #> Loss function used:          brier #> Optimized kernel parameters: 0.0386 #> Minimum achieved loss:       0.00617 #> Kernel parameters were obtained by optimization. #>  #> Prior specification: #>   Noninformative prior: Beta(1,1). #> --------------------------------------------------   #-------------------------- 2D Example --------------------------- set.seed(123)  # Define 2D latent function and probability transformation true_pi_fun <- function(X) {   if(is.null(nrow(X))) X <- matrix(X, nrow=1)   m <- 8.6928   s <- 2.4269   x1 <- 4*X[,1]- 2   x2 <- 4*X[,2]- 2   a <- 1 + (x1 + x2 + 1)^2 *     (19- 14*x1 + 3*x1^2- 14*x2 + 6*x1*x2 + 3*x2^2)   b <- 30 + (2*x1- 3*x2)^2 *     (18- 32*x1 + 12*x1^2 + 48*x2- 36*x1*x2 + 27*x2^2)   f <- log(a*b)   f <- (f- m)/s   return(pnorm(f))  # Transform to probability }  n <- 100 Xbounds <- matrix(c(0, 0, 1, 1), nrow = 2) X <- tgp::lhs(n = n, rect = Xbounds) true_pi <- true_pi_fun(X) m <- sample(100, n, replace = TRUE) y <- rbinom(n, size = m, prob = true_pi)  # Fit BKP model model2 <- fit.BKP(X, y, m, Xbounds=Xbounds) print(model2) #> -------------------------------------------------- #>            Beta Kernel Process (BKP) Model         #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Number of observations (n):  100 #> Input dimensionality (d):    2 #> Kernel type:                 gaussian #> Loss function used:          brier #> Optimized kernel parameters: 0.1112, 0.0680 #> Minimum achieved loss:       0.01041 #> Kernel parameters were obtained by optimization. #>  #> Prior specification: #>   Noninformative prior: Beta(1,1). #> --------------------------------------------------

what: Construct Prior Parameters for the BKP Model — get_prior
title: Construct Prior Parameters for the BKP Model — get_prior
text: Computes prior Beta distribution parameters alpha0 beta0 input location, based chosen prior specification. Supports noninformative, fixed, data-adaptive prior strategies.

title: Construct Prior Parameters for the BKP Model — get_prior
code: get_prior(   prior = c("noninformative", "fixed", "adaptive"),   r0 = 2,   p0 = 0.5,   y = NULL,   m = NULL,   K = NULL )

title: Construct Prior Parameters for the BKP Model — get_prior
text: prior Character string specifying type prior use. One "noninformative", "fixed", "adaptive". r0 Positive scalar indicating global precision parameter. Used prior "fixed" "adaptive". p0 Prior mean success probability ((0,1)). Used prior = "fixed". y Numeric vector observed successes, length n. m Numeric vector total binomial trials, length n. K precomputed kernel matrix size n × n, typically obtained kernel_matrix.

title: Construct Prior Parameters for the BKP Model — get_prior
text: list two numeric vectors: alpha0 Prior alpha parameters Beta distribution, length n. beta0 Prior beta parameters Beta distribution, length n.

title: Construct Prior Parameters for the BKP Model — get_prior
text: prior = "noninformative", prior parameters set 1 (noninformative prior). prior = "fixed", locations share Beta prior: Beta(r0 * p0, r0 * (1 - p0)). prior = "adaptive", prior mean location computed kernel smoothing observed proportions y/m, precision r0 distributed accordingly.

title: Construct Prior Parameters for the BKP Model — get_prior
code: # Simulated data set.seed(123) n <- 10 X <- matrix(runif(n * 2), ncol = 2) y <- rbinom(n, size = 5, prob = 0.6) m <- rep(5, n)  # Example kernel matrix (Gaussian) K <- kernel_matrix(X)  # Construct adaptive prior prior <- get_prior(prior = "adaptive", r0 = 2, y = y, m = m, K = K)

what: Construct Prior Parameters for the DKP Model — get_prior_dkp
title: Construct Prior Parameters for the DKP Model — get_prior_dkp
text: Computes prior Dirichlet distribution parameters alpha0 input location Dirichlet Kernel Process model, based specified prior type: noninformative, fixed, adaptive.

title: Construct Prior Parameters for the DKP Model — get_prior_dkp
code: get_prior_dkp(   prior = c("noninformative", "fixed", "adaptive"),   r0 = 2,   p0 = NULL,   Y = NULL,   K = NULL )

title: Construct Prior Parameters for the DKP Model — get_prior_dkp
text: prior Character string specifying type prior use. One "noninformative", "fixed", "adaptive". r0 Positive scalar indicating global precision parameter. Used prior "fixed" "adaptive". p0 Numeric vector specifying global prior mean class (must sum 1). used prior = "fixed". length equal number classes. Y Numeric matrix observed class counts size n × q, n number observations q number classes. K precomputed kernel matrix size n × n, typically obtained kernel_matrix.

title: Construct Prior Parameters for the DKP Model — get_prior_dkp
text: list containing: alpha0 numeric matrix prior Dirichlet parameters input location; dimension n × q.

title: Construct Prior Parameters for the DKP Model — get_prior_dkp
text: prior = "noninformative", entries alpha0 set 1 (flat Dirichlet). prior = "fixed", rows alpha0 set r0 * p0. prior = "adaptive", row alpha0 computed kernel-weighted smoothing observed relative frequencies Y, scaled r0.

title: Construct Prior Parameters for the DKP Model — get_prior_dkp
code: # Simulated multi-class data set.seed(123) n <- 15           # number of training points q <- 3            # number of classes X <- matrix(runif(n * 2), ncol = 2)  # Simulate class probabilities and draw multinomial counts true_pi <- t(apply(X, 1, function(x) {   raw <- c(     exp(-sum((x - 0.2)^2)),     exp(-sum((x - 0.5)^2)),     exp(-sum((x - 0.8)^2))   )   raw / sum(raw) })) m <- sample(10:20, n, replace = TRUE) Y <- t(sapply(1:n, function(i) rmultinom(1, size = m[i], prob = true_pi[i, ])))  # Compute kernel matrix (Gaussian) K <- kernel_matrix(X, theta = rep(0.2, 2), kernel = "gaussian")  # Construct adaptive prior prior_dkp <- get_prior_dkp(prior = "adaptive", r0 = 2, Y = Y, K = K)

what: Compute Kernel Matrix Between Input Locations — kernel_matrix
title: Compute Kernel Matrix Between Input Locations — kernel_matrix
text: Computes kernel matrix two sets input locations using specified kernel function. Supports isotropic anisotropic lengthscales. Available kernels include Gaussian, Matérn 5/2, Matérn 3/2.

title: Compute Kernel Matrix Between Input Locations — kernel_matrix
code: kernel_matrix(   X,   Xprime = NULL,   theta = 0.1,   kernel = c("gaussian", "matern52", "matern32"),   anisotropic = TRUE )

title: Compute Kernel Matrix Between Input Locations — kernel_matrix
text: X numeric matrix (vector) input locations shape \(n \times d\). Xprime optional numeric matrix input locations shape \(m   \times d\). NULL (default), set X, resulting symmetric matrix. theta positive numeric value vector specifying kernel lengthscale(s). scalar, lengthscale applied input dimensions. vector, must length d, corresponding anisotropic scaling. kernel character string specifying kernel function. Must one "gaussian", "matern32", "matern52". anisotropic Logical. TRUE (default), theta interpreted vector per-dimension lengthscales. FALSE, theta treated scalar.

title: Compute Kernel Matrix Between Input Locations — kernel_matrix
text: numeric matrix size \(n \times m\), element \(K_{ij}\) gives kernel similarity input \(X_i\) \(X'_j\).

title: Compute Kernel Matrix Between Input Locations — kernel_matrix
text: Let \(\mathbf{x}\) \(\mathbf{x}'\) denote two input points. scaled distance defined $$      r = \left\| \frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}'}{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \right\|_2.   $$ available kernels defined : Gaussian: $$        k(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \exp(-r^2)      $$ Matérn 5/2: $$        k(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \left(1 + \sqrt{5} r + \frac{5}{3} r^2 \right) \exp(-\sqrt{5} r)      $$ Matérn 3/2: $$        k(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \left(1 + \sqrt{3} r \right) \exp(-\sqrt{3} r)      $$ function performs consistency checks input dimensions automatically broadcasts theta scalar.

title: Compute Kernel Matrix Between Input Locations — kernel_matrix
text: Rasmussen, C. E., & Williams, C. K. . (2006). Gaussian Processes Machine Learning. MIT Press.

title: Compute Kernel Matrix Between Input Locations — kernel_matrix
code: # Basic usage with default Xprime = X X <- matrix(runif(20), ncol = 2) K1 <- kernel_matrix(X, theta = 0.2, kernel = "gaussian")  # Anisotropic lengthscales with Matérn 5/2 K2 <- kernel_matrix(X, theta = c(0.1, 0.3), kernel = "matern52")  # Isotropic Matérn 3/2 K3 <- kernel_matrix(X, theta = 1, kernel = "matern32", anisotropic = FALSE)  # Use Xprime different from X Xprime <- matrix(runif(10), ncol = 2) K4 <- kernel_matrix(X, Xprime, theta = 0.2, kernel = "gaussian")

what: Loss Function for Fitting the BKP Model — loss_fun
title: Loss Function for Fitting the BKP Model — loss_fun
text: Computes loss used fit BKP model. Supports Brier score (mean squared error) negative log-loss (cross-entropy), different prior specifications.

title: Loss Function for Fitting the BKP Model — loss_fun
code: loss_fun(   gamma,   Xnorm,   y,   m,   prior = c("noninformative", "fixed", "adaptive"),   r0 = 2,   p0 = 0.5,   loss = c("brier", "log_loss"),   kernel = c("gaussian", "matern52", "matern32") )

title: Loss Function for Fitting the BKP Model — loss_fun
text: gamma numeric vector log-transformed kernel hyperparameters. Xnorm numeric matrix normalized inputs (column scaled [0,1]). y numeric vector observed successes (length n). m numeric vector total binomial trials (length n), corresponding y. prior Type prior use. One "noninformative", "fixed", "adaptive". r0 Global prior precision (used prior = "fixed" "adaptive"). p0 Global prior mean (used prior = "fixed"). loss Loss function kernel hyperparameter tuning. One "brier" (default) "log_loss". kernel Kernel function local weighting. Choose "gaussian", "matern52", "matern32".

title: Loss Function for Fitting the BKP Model — loss_fun
text: single numeric value representing total loss (minimized).

title: Loss Function for Fitting the BKP Model — loss_fun
code: set.seed(123) n = 10 Xnorm = matrix(runif(2 * n), ncol = 2) m = rep(10, n) y = rbinom(n, size = m, prob = runif(n)) loss_fun(gamma = rep(0, 2), Xnorm = Xnorm, y = y, m = m) #> [1] 0.09313263

what: Loss Function for Fitting the DKP Model — loss_fun_dkp
title: Loss Function for Fitting the DKP Model — loss_fun_dkp
text: Computes loss used fit DKP model. Supports Brier score (mean squared error) negative log-loss (cross-entropy), different prior specifications.

title: Loss Function for Fitting the DKP Model — loss_fun_dkp
code: loss_fun_dkp(   gamma,   Xnorm,   Y,   prior = c("noninformative", "fixed", "adaptive"),   r0 = 2,   p0 = NULL,   loss = c("brier", "log_loss"),   kernel = c("gaussian", "matern52", "matern32") )

title: Loss Function for Fitting the DKP Model — loss_fun_dkp
text: gamma numeric vector log-transformed kernel hyperparameters. Xnorm numeric matrix normalized inputs (column scaled [0,1]). Y Matrix observed multinomial counts, dimension \(n \times q\). prior Type prior use. One "noninformative", "fixed", "adaptive". r0 Global prior precision (used prior = "fixed" "adaptive"). p0 Global prior mean vector (used prior = "fixed"). Must length \(q\). loss Loss function kernel hyperparameter tuning. One "brier" (default) "log_loss". kernel Kernel function local weighting. Choose "gaussian", "matern52", "matern32".

title: Loss Function for Fitting the DKP Model — loss_fun_dkp
text: single numeric value representing total loss (minimized).

title: Loss Function for Fitting the DKP Model — loss_fun_dkp
code: set.seed(123) n = 10 Xnorm = matrix(runif(2 * n), ncol = 2) m = rep(10, n) y = rbinom(n, size = m, prob = runif(n)) Y = cbind(y, m-y) loss_fun_dkp(gamma = rep(0, 2), Xnorm = Xnorm, Y = Y) #> [1] 0.09313263

what: Plot Fitted BKP or DKP Models — plot
title: Plot Fitted BKP or DKP Models — plot
text: Visualizes fitted BKP DKP models depending input dimensionality. 1-dimensional inputs, displays predicted class probabilities credible intervals observed data. 2-dimensional inputs, generates contour plots posterior summaries.

title: Plot Fitted BKP or DKP Models — plot
code: # S3 method for class 'BKP' plot(x, only_mean = FALSE, ...)  # S3 method for class 'DKP' plot(x, only_mean = FALSE, ...)

title: Plot Fitted BKP or DKP Models — plot
text: x object class "BKP" "DKP", typically returned fit.BKP fit.DKP. only_mean Logical. TRUE, predicted mean surface plotted 2D inputs (applies BKP models). Default FALSE. ... Additional arguments passed internal plotting routines (currently unused).

title: Plot Fitted BKP or DKP Models — plot
text: function return value. called side effects, producing plots visualize model predictions uncertainty.

title: Plot Fitted BKP or DKP Models — plot
text: plotting behavior depends dimensionality input covariates: 1D inputs: BKP (binary/binomial data), plots posterior mean curve 95% credible band, overlaid observed proportions (\(y/m\)). DKP (categorical/multinomial data), plots one curve per class, shaded credible interval observed class frequencies. classification tasks, optional curve maximum posterior class probability can displayed visualize decision confidence. 2D inputs: models, produces either: predictive mean surface (optionally maximum posterior probability classification), 2--2 panel contour plots showing: predictive mean, predictive 97.5th percentile (upper bound 95% credible interval), predictive variance, predictive 2.5th percentile (lower bound). DKP, surfaces generated separately class. input dimensions greater two, function terminates error message.

title: Plot Fitted BKP or DKP Models — plot
code: # ============================================================== # # ========================= BKP Examples ======================= # # ============================================================== #  #-------------------------- 1D Example --------------------------- set.seed(123)  # Define true success probability function true_pi_fun <- function(x) {   (1 + exp(-x^2) * cos(10 * (1 - exp(-x)) / (1 + exp(-x)))) / 2 }  n <- 30 Xbounds <- matrix(c(-2,2), nrow=1) X <- tgp::lhs(n = n, rect = Xbounds) true_pi <- true_pi_fun(X) m <- sample(100, n, replace = TRUE) y <- rbinom(n, size = m, prob = true_pi)  # Fit BKP model model1 <- fit.BKP(X, y, m, Xbounds=Xbounds)  # Plot results plot(model1)    #-------------------------- 2D Example --------------------------- set.seed(123)  # Define 2D latent function and probability transformation true_pi_fun <- function(X) {   if(is.null(nrow(X))) X <- matrix(X, nrow=1)   m <- 8.6928   s <- 2.4269   x1 <- 4*X[,1]- 2   x2 <- 4*X[,2]- 2   a <- 1 + (x1 + x2 + 1)^2 *     (19- 14*x1 + 3*x1^2- 14*x2 + 6*x1*x2 + 3*x2^2)   b <- 30 + (2*x1- 3*x2)^2 *     (18- 32*x1 + 12*x1^2 + 48*x2- 36*x1*x2 + 27*x2^2)   f <- log(a*b)   f <- (f- m)/s   return(pnorm(f))  # Transform to probability }  n <- 100 Xbounds <- matrix(c(0, 0, 1, 1), nrow = 2) X <- tgp::lhs(n = n, rect = Xbounds) true_pi <- true_pi_fun(X) m <- sample(100, n, replace = TRUE) y <- rbinom(n, size = m, prob = true_pi)  # Fit BKP model model2 <- fit.BKP(X, y, m, Xbounds=Xbounds)  # Plot results plot(model2)   # ============================================================== # # ========================= DKP Examples ======================= # # ============================================================== #  #-------------------------- 1D Example --------------------------- set.seed(123)  # Define true class probability function (3-class) true_pi_fun <- function(X) {   p <- (1 + exp(-X^2) * cos(10 * (1 - exp(-X)) / (1 + exp(-X)))) / 2   return(matrix(c(p/2, p/2, 1 - p), nrow = length(p))) }  n <- 30 Xbounds <- matrix(c(-2, 2), nrow = 1) X <- tgp::lhs(n = n, rect = Xbounds) true_pi <- true_pi_fun(X) m <- sample(100, n, replace = TRUE)  # Generate multinomial responses Y <- t(sapply(1:n, function(i) rmultinom(1, size = m[i], prob = true_pi[i, ])))  # Fit DKP model model1 <- fit.DKP(X, Y, Xbounds = Xbounds)  # Plot results plot(model1)   #-------------------------- 2D Example --------------------------- set.seed(123)  # Define latent function and transform to 3-class probabilities true_pi_fun <- function(X) {   if (is.null(nrow(X))) X <- matrix(X, nrow = 1)   m <- 8.6928; s <- 2.4269   x1 <- 4 * X[,1] - 2   x2 <- 4 * X[,2] - 2   a <- 1 + (x1 + x2 + 1)^2 *     (19 - 14*x1 + 3*x1^2 - 14*x2 + 6*x1*x2 + 3*x2^2)   b <- 30 + (2*x1 - 3*x2)^2 *     (18 - 32*x1 + 12*x1^2 + 48*x2 - 36*x1*x2 + 27*x2^2)   f <- (log(a * b) - m) / s   p <- pnorm(f)   return(matrix(c(p/2, p/2, 1 - p), nrow = length(p))) }  n <- 100 Xbounds <- matrix(c(0, 0, 1, 1), nrow = 2) X <- tgp::lhs(n = n, rect = Xbounds) true_pi <- true_pi_fun(X) m <- sample(100, n, replace = TRUE)  # Generate multinomial responses Y <- t(sapply(1:n, function(i) rmultinom(1, size = m[i], prob = true_pi[i, ])))  # Fit DKP model model2 <- fit.DKP(X, Y, Xbounds = Xbounds)  # Plot results plot(model2)

what: Predict Method for BKP or DKP Models — predict
title: Predict Method for BKP or DKP Models — predict
text: Generates posterior predictive summaries fitted BKP DKP model new input locations.

title: Predict Method for BKP or DKP Models — predict
code: # S3 method for class 'BKP' predict(object, Xnew, CI_level = 0.95, threshold = 0.5, ...)  # S3 method for class 'DKP' predict(object, Xnew, CI_level = 0.95, ...)

title: Predict Method for BKP or DKP Models — predict
text: object object class "BKP" "DKP", typically returned fit.BKP fit.DKP. Xnew numeric matrix (vector) new input locations predictions desired. CI_level Credible level prediction intervals (default 0.95, corresponding 95% CI). threshold Classification threshold binary prediction based posterior mean (used BKP; default 0.5). ... Additional arguments passed generic predict methods (currently used; included S3 method consistency).

title: Predict Method for BKP or DKP Models — predict
text: list following components: Xnew new input locations. mean BKP: Posterior mean success probability location. DKP: matrix posterior mean class probabilities (rows = inputs, columns = classes). variance BKP: Posterior variance success probability. DKP: matrix posterior variances class. lower BKP: Lower bound prediction interval (e.g., 2.5th percentile 95% CI). DKP: matrix lower bounds class (e.g., 2.5th percentile). upper BKP: Upper bound prediction interval (e.g., 97.5th percentile 95% CI). DKP: matrix upper bounds class (e.g., 97.5th percentile). class BKP: Predicted binary label (0 1), based posterior mean threshold, m = 1. DKP: Predicted class label (.e., class highest posterior mean probability).

title: Predict Method for BKP or DKP Models — predict

what: Print Summary of a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — print
title: Print Summary of a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — print
text: Displays concise summary fitted BKP DKP model. output includes key characteristics sample size, input dimensionality, kernel type, loss function, optimized kernel hyperparameters, minimum loss.

title: Print Summary of a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — print
code: # S3 method for class 'BKP' print(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'DKP' print(x, ...)

title: Print Summary of a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — print
text: x object class "BKP" (fit.BKP) "DKP" (fit.DKP). ... Additional arguments passed generic print method (currently used).

title: Print Summary of a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — print
text: Invisibly returns input object (class "BKP" "DKP"). function called side effect printing summary console.

title: Print Summary of a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — print
code: # ============================================================== # # ========================= BKP Examples ======================= # # ============================================================== #  #-------------------------- 1D Example --------------------------- set.seed(123)  # Define true success probability function true_pi_fun <- function(x) {   (1 + exp(-x^2) * cos(10 * (1 - exp(-x)) / (1 + exp(-x)))) / 2 }  n <- 30 Xbounds <- matrix(c(-2,2), nrow=1) X <- tgp::lhs(n = n, rect = Xbounds) true_pi <- true_pi_fun(X) m <- sample(100, n, replace = TRUE) y <- rbinom(n, size = m, prob = true_pi)  # Fit BKP model model1 <- fit.BKP(X, y, m, Xbounds=Xbounds) print(model1) #> -------------------------------------------------- #>            Beta Kernel Process (BKP) Model         #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Number of observations (n):  30 #> Input dimensionality (d):    1 #> Kernel type:                 gaussian #> Loss function used:          brier #> Optimized kernel parameters: 0.0386 #> Minimum achieved loss:       0.00617 #> Kernel parameters were obtained by optimization. #>  #> Prior specification: #>   Noninformative prior: Beta(1,1). #> --------------------------------------------------   #-------------------------- 2D Example --------------------------- set.seed(123)  # Define 2D latent function and probability transformation true_pi_fun <- function(X) {   if(is.null(nrow(X))) X <- matrix(X, nrow=1)   m <- 8.6928   s <- 2.4269   x1 <- 4*X[,1]- 2   x2 <- 4*X[,2]- 2   a <- 1 + (x1 + x2 + 1)^2 *     (19- 14*x1 + 3*x1^2- 14*x2 + 6*x1*x2 + 3*x2^2)   b <- 30 + (2*x1- 3*x2)^2 *     (18- 32*x1 + 12*x1^2 + 48*x2- 36*x1*x2 + 27*x2^2)   f <- log(a*b)   f <- (f- m)/s   return(pnorm(f))  # Transform to probability }  n <- 100 Xbounds <- matrix(c(0, 0, 1, 1), nrow = 2) X <- tgp::lhs(n = n, rect = Xbounds) true_pi <- true_pi_fun(X) m <- sample(100, n, replace = TRUE) y <- rbinom(n, size = m, prob = true_pi)  # Fit BKP model model2 <- fit.BKP(X, y, m, Xbounds=Xbounds) print(model2) #> -------------------------------------------------- #>            Beta Kernel Process (BKP) Model         #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Number of observations (n):  100 #> Input dimensionality (d):    2 #> Kernel type:                 gaussian #> Loss function used:          brier #> Optimized kernel parameters: 0.1112, 0.0680 #> Minimum achieved loss:       0.01041 #> Kernel parameters were obtained by optimization. #>  #> Prior specification: #>   Noninformative prior: Beta(1,1). #> --------------------------------------------------  # ============================================================== # # ========================= DKP Examples ======================= # # ============================================================== #  #-------------------------- 1D Example --------------------------- set.seed(123)  # Define true class probability function (3-class) true_pi_fun <- function(X) {   p <- (1 + exp(-X^2) * cos(10 * (1 - exp(-X)) / (1 + exp(-X)))) / 2   return(matrix(c(p/2, p/2, 1 - p), nrow = length(p))) }  n <- 30 Xbounds <- matrix(c(-2, 2), nrow = 1) X <- tgp::lhs(n = n, rect = Xbounds) true_pi <- true_pi_fun(X) m <- sample(100, n, replace = TRUE)  # Generate multinomial responses Y <- t(sapply(1:n, function(i) rmultinom(1, size = m[i], prob = true_pi[i, ])))  # Fit DKP model model1 <- fit.DKP(X, Y, Xbounds = Xbounds) print(model1) #> -------------------------------------------------- #>            Dirichlet Kernel Process (DKP) Model         #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Number of observations (n):  30 #> Input dimensionality (d):    1 #> Output dimensionality (q):   3 #> Kernel type:                 gaussian #> Loss function used:          brier #> Optimized kernel parameters: 0.0407 #> Minimum achieved loss:       0.01007 #> Kernel parameters were obtained by optimization. #>  #> Prior specification: #>   Noninformative prior: Dirichlet(1,...,1). #> --------------------------------------------------   #-------------------------- 2D Example --------------------------- set.seed(123)  # Define latent function and transform to 3-class probabilities true_pi_fun <- function(X) {   if (is.null(nrow(X))) X <- matrix(X, nrow = 1)   m <- 8.6928; s <- 2.4269   x1 <- 4 * X[,1] - 2   x2 <- 4 * X[,2] - 2   a <- 1 + (x1 + x2 + 1)^2 *     (19 - 14*x1 + 3*x1^2 - 14*x2 + 6*x1*x2 + 3*x2^2)   b <- 30 + (2*x1 - 3*x2)^2 *     (18 - 32*x1 + 12*x1^2 + 48*x2 - 36*x1*x2 + 27*x2^2)   f <- (log(a * b) - m) / s   p <- pnorm(f)   return(matrix(c(p/2, p/2, 1 - p), nrow = length(p))) }  n <- 100 Xbounds <- matrix(c(0, 0, 1, 1), nrow = 2) X <- tgp::lhs(n = n, rect = Xbounds) true_pi <- true_pi_fun(X) m <- sample(100, n, replace = TRUE)  # Generate multinomial responses Y <- t(sapply(1:n, function(i) rmultinom(1, size = m[i], prob = true_pi[i, ])))  # Fit DKP model model2 <- fit.DKP(X, Y, Xbounds = Xbounds) print(model2) #> -------------------------------------------------- #>            Dirichlet Kernel Process (DKP) Model         #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Number of observations (n):  100 #> Input dimensionality (d):    2 #> Output dimensionality (q):   3 #> Kernel type:                 gaussian #> Loss function used:          brier #> Optimized kernel parameters: 0.1245, 0.0706 #> Minimum achieved loss:       0.01060 #> Kernel parameters were obtained by optimization. #>  #> Prior specification: #>   Noninformative prior: Dirichlet(1,...,1). #> --------------------------------------------------

what: Simulate from a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — simulate
title: Simulate from a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — simulate
text: Generates random samples posterior predictive distribution fitted BKP DKP model new input locations. BKP models, posterior samples drawn Beta distributions representing success probabilities, optional binary class labels determined threshold. DKP models, posterior samples drawn Dirichlet distributions representing class probabilities, optional class labels determined maximum posteriori (MAP) rule training responses one-hot encoded.

title: Simulate from a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — simulate
code: # S3 method for class 'BKP' simulate(object, Xnew, n_sim = 1, threshold = NULL, seed = NULL, ...)  # S3 method for class 'DKP' simulate(object, Xnew, n_sim = 1, seed = NULL, ...)

title: Simulate from a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — simulate
text: object object class "BKP" "DKP", typically returned fit.BKP fit.DKP. Xnew numeric matrix vector new input locations simulation. n_sim Number posterior samples generate (default = 1). threshold Classification threshold binary output (used BKP). specified, output include binary class labels values threshold classified 1 (default NULL). seed Optional integer seed reproducibility. ... Additional arguments (currently unused).

title: Simulate from a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — simulate
text: list following components: sims BKP: numeric matrix dimension nrow(Xnew) × n_sim, containing simulated success probabilities. DKP: numeric array dimension n_sim × q × nrow(Xnew), containing simulated class probabilities Dirichlet posteriors, q number classes. mean BKP: numeric vector posterior mean success probabilities Xnew. DKP: numeric matrix dimension nrow(Xnew) × q, containing posterior mean class probabilities. class BKP: binary matrix dimension nrow(Xnew) × n_sim indicating simulated class labels (0/1), returned threshold specified. DKP: numeric matrix dimension nrow(Xnew) × n_sim containing MAP-predicted class labels, returned training data single-label (.e., row Y sums 1).

title: Simulate from a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — simulate

what: Summary of a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — summary
title: Summary of a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — summary
text: Provides summary fitted Beta Kernel Process (BKP) Dirichlet Kernel Process (DKP) model. Currently, function acts wrapper print.BKP print.DKP, delivering concise overview key model characteristics fitting results.

title: Summary of a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — summary
code: # S3 method for class 'BKP' summary(object, ...)  # S3 method for class 'DKP' summary(object, ...)

title: Summary of a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — summary
text: object object class "BKP" (fit.BKP) "DKP" (fit.DKP). ... Additional arguments passed generic summary method (currently used).

title: Summary of a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — summary
text: Invisibly returns input object (class "BKP" "DKP"). Called side effects: prints concise summary fitted model.

title: Summary of a Fitted BKP or DKP Model — summary
code: # ============================================================== # # ========================= BKP Examples ======================= # # ============================================================== #  #-------------------------- 1D Example --------------------------- set.seed(123)  # Define true success probability function true_pi_fun <- function(x) {   (1 + exp(-x^2) * cos(10 * (1 - exp(-x)) / (1 + exp(-x)))) / 2 }  n <- 30 Xbounds <- matrix(c(-2,2), nrow=1) X <- tgp::lhs(n = n, rect = Xbounds) true_pi <- true_pi_fun(X) m <- sample(100, n, replace = TRUE) y <- rbinom(n, size = m, prob = true_pi)  # Fit BKP model model1 <- fit.BKP(X, y, m, Xbounds=Xbounds) summary(model1) #> -------------------------------------------------- #>            Beta Kernel Process (BKP) Model         #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Number of observations (n):  30 #> Input dimensionality (d):    1 #> Kernel type:                 gaussian #> Loss function used:          brier #> Optimized kernel parameters: 0.0386 #> Minimum achieved loss:       0.00617 #> Kernel parameters were obtained by optimization. #>  #> Prior specification: #>   Noninformative prior: Beta(1,1). #> --------------------------------------------------   #-------------------------- 2D Example --------------------------- set.seed(123)  # Define 2D latent function and probability transformation true_pi_fun <- function(X) {   if(is.null(nrow(X))) X <- matrix(X, nrow=1)   m <- 8.6928   s <- 2.4269   x1 <- 4*X[,1]- 2   x2 <- 4*X[,2]- 2   a <- 1 + (x1 + x2 + 1)^2 *     (19- 14*x1 + 3*x1^2- 14*x2 + 6*x1*x2 + 3*x2^2)   b <- 30 + (2*x1- 3*x2)^2 *     (18- 32*x1 + 12*x1^2 + 48*x2- 36*x1*x2 + 27*x2^2)   f <- log(a*b)   f <- (f- m)/s   return(pnorm(f))  # Transform to probability }  n <- 100 Xbounds <- matrix(c(0, 0, 1, 1), nrow = 2) X <- tgp::lhs(n = n, rect = Xbounds) true_pi <- true_pi_fun(X) m <- sample(100, n, replace = TRUE) y <- rbinom(n, size = m, prob = true_pi)  # Fit BKP model model2 <- fit.BKP(X, y, m, Xbounds=Xbounds) summary(model2) #> -------------------------------------------------- #>            Beta Kernel Process (BKP) Model         #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Number of observations (n):  100 #> Input dimensionality (d):    2 #> Kernel type:                 gaussian #> Loss function used:          brier #> Optimized kernel parameters: 0.1112, 0.0680 #> Minimum achieved loss:       0.01041 #> Kernel parameters were obtained by optimization. #>  #> Prior specification: #>   Noninformative prior: Beta(1,1). #> --------------------------------------------------  # ============================================================== # # ========================= DKP Examples ======================= # # ============================================================== #  #-------------------------- 1D Example --------------------------- set.seed(123)  # Define true class probability function (3-class) true_pi_fun <- function(X) {   p <- (1 + exp(-X^2) * cos(10 * (1 - exp(-X)) / (1 + exp(-X)))) / 2   return(matrix(c(p/2, p/2, 1 - p), nrow = length(p))) }  n <- 30 Xbounds <- matrix(c(-2, 2), nrow = 1) X <- tgp::lhs(n = n, rect = Xbounds) true_pi <- true_pi_fun(X) m <- sample(100, n, replace = TRUE)  # Generate multinomial responses Y <- t(sapply(1:n, function(i) rmultinom(1, size = m[i], prob = true_pi[i, ])))  # Fit DKP model model1 <- fit.DKP(X, Y, Xbounds = Xbounds) summary(model1) #> -------------------------------------------------- #>            Dirichlet Kernel Process (DKP) Model         #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Number of observations (n):  30 #> Input dimensionality (d):    1 #> Output dimensionality (q):   3 #> Kernel type:                 gaussian #> Loss function used:          brier #> Optimized kernel parameters: 0.0407 #> Minimum achieved loss:       0.01007 #> Kernel parameters were obtained by optimization. #>  #> Prior specification: #>   Noninformative prior: Dirichlet(1,...,1). #> --------------------------------------------------   #-------------------------- 2D Example --------------------------- set.seed(123)  # Define latent function and transform to 3-class probabilities true_pi_fun <- function(X) {   if (is.null(nrow(X))) X <- matrix(X, nrow = 1)   m <- 8.6928; s <- 2.4269   x1 <- 4 * X[,1] - 2   x2 <- 4 * X[,2] - 2   a <- 1 + (x1 + x2 + 1)^2 *     (19 - 14*x1 + 3*x1^2 - 14*x2 + 6*x1*x2 + 3*x2^2)   b <- 30 + (2*x1 - 3*x2)^2 *     (18 - 32*x1 + 12*x1^2 + 48*x2 - 36*x1*x2 + 27*x2^2)   f <- (log(a * b) - m) / s   p <- pnorm(f)   return(matrix(c(p/2, p/2, 1 - p), nrow = length(p))) }  n <- 100 Xbounds <- matrix(c(0, 0, 1, 1), nrow = 2) X <- tgp::lhs(n = n, rect = Xbounds) true_pi <- true_pi_fun(X) m <- sample(100, n, replace = TRUE)  # Generate multinomial responses Y <- t(sapply(1:n, function(i) rmultinom(1, size = m[i], prob = true_pi[i, ])))  # Fit DKP model model2 <- fit.DKP(X, Y, Xbounds = Xbounds) summary(model2) #> -------------------------------------------------- #>            Dirichlet Kernel Process (DKP) Model         #> -------------------------------------------------- #> Number of observations (n):  100 #> Input dimensionality (d):    2 #> Output dimensionality (q):   3 #> Kernel type:                 gaussian #> Loss function used:          brier #> Optimized kernel parameters: 0.1245, 0.0706 #> Minimum achieved loss:       0.01060 #> Kernel parameters were obtained by optimization. #>  #> Prior specification: #>   Noninformative prior: Dirichlet(1,...,1). #> --------------------------------------------------

title: BKP 0.1.0 (2025-07-23)
text: CRAN release: 2025-07-23 Initial CRAN submission.
